Review on machine learning-based bioprocess optimization, monitoring, and control systems
=========================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Partha Pratim Mondal a 1, Abhinav Galodha b 1, Vishal Kumar Verma a, Vijai Singh c, Pau Loke Show d e f, [Mukesh Kumar Awasthi g](https://www.sciencedirect.com/author/13003468000/mukesh-kumar-kumar-awasthi), Brejesh Lall h, Sanya Anees i, Katrin Pollmann j, Rohan Jain j

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2022.128523](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2022.128523 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0960852422018569&orderBeanReset=true)

Highlights
----------

*   •A facile conceptual introduction to the basics of ML for bioprocessing applications. 
*   •Exploration of ML algorithms in Pharmaceutical, biofuel, and [wastewater treatment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/wastewater-treatment "Learn more about wastewater treatment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). 
*   •Hybrid model for multi-dimensional aspects & better prediction capabilities. 
*   •[ANN](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/neural-network "Learn more about ANN from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), GA, RSM, and [SVM](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/support-vector-machine "Learn more about SVM from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") models dominate biofuel and water treatment. 
*   •PLS and [ANN](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/neural-network "Learn more about ANN from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are the most used algorithm in biopharmaceutical production. 

Abstract
--------

Machine Learning is quickly becoming an impending game changer for transforming big data thrust from the bioprocessing industry into actionable output. However, the complex data set from bioprocess, lagging cyber-integrated sensor system, and issues with storage scalability limit machine learning real-time application. Hence, it is imperative to know the state of technology to address prevailing issues. This review first gives an insight into the basic understanding of the machine learning domain and discusses its complexities for more comprehensive applications. Followed by an outline of how relevant machine learning models are for statistical and logical analysis of the enormous datasets generated to control bioprocess operations. Then this review critically discusses the current knowledge, its limitations, and future aspects in different subfields of the bioprocessing industry. Further, this review discusses the prospects of adopting a hybrid method to dovetail different modeling strategies, cyber-networking, and integrated sensors to develop new digital biotechnologies.

------------------

2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0960852422018569-ga1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Introduction
------------

The recent pandemic and climate change events have forced world governments to focus on better healthcare, self-sustainability, circular economy, and clean industrial growth. A significant development in this regard has been observed in the bioprocessing industry, where the production of new bioproducts and bioprocesses has increased manifold (Al-Asheh et al., 2021, Joshi and Mishra, 2022, Sarwal et al., 2021). These developments are primarily relevant to bioprocessing subfields such as biopharmaceuticals/biotherapeutics production, biofuel production, and biological wastewater treatment processes where the demands have never been greater. To ensure these bioproduct development processes' commercial economics and sustainability, they must be planned and executed synchronously throughout the production lifecycle. The biotech industry is undergoing a digital transformation to overcome these constraints, with the adoption of innovative technologies like artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) as the top priority for automation of the involved production process (Puranik et al., 2022, Rathore et al., 2022a, Rathore et al., 2022b). AI-based ML techniques develop, monitor, control, and optimize process systems. They are efficient at learning complex relationships between process parameters and performance (Mowbray et al., 2021). ML can predict and influence the critical process parameters (CPP) and product critical quality attributes (CQAs), control process systems in response to parameter deviations and comprehend complete data analysis from the manufacturing process (Walsh et al., 2022).

A typical bioprocess development is based on optimizing a sequence of unit operations to efficiently maximize the production, enhance the recovery and purify a specific bioproduct with desired yield and purity (Fernández-Lucas et al., 2021). A variety of factors like technical understanding of involved bio-kinetics, time-delayed and scale-dependent bioprocess responses, decelerating process drifts, instrumentation, process disturbances, and environmental disturbances may influence process outcome. Usually, a substantial number of experiments are needed to study the comprehensive effect of these influencing factors that consume lots of processing medium and time. At this stage, modeling and computer-assisted tools are potent methods for enhancing bioprocess yield (Heinzle et al., 2007). US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and European Medicines Agency (EMA) recommend a Quality by Design (QbD) approach for product quality assurance in the bioprocessing and biopharmaceutical industry (Rathore and Winkle, 2009). In the QbD paradigm, CQAs of product and related yield are viewed as a function of CPPs. In this approach, the experiment design (DoE) is framed structurally by varying CPPs to evaluate the corresponding effect on product yield and CQAs. The datasets then generated are used for projecting complex relationships between CPPs and CQAs for implementation and execution of the QbD paradigm. These process models provide the relationships between the input, output, and system operational variables to effectively describe the process phenomena and the influencing factors for control, optimization, and a better understanding of significant decision-making (Walsh et al., 2022).

Any industrial bioprocess system deals with the collection and handling of enormously extensive online and offline data generated at each unit operation. The production processes data from multiple sources like process control systems, bioreactor conditions, sensors, applied analytical methods, omics data, material input, and process output. Big bioprocessing industries are now investing in data management via ML, cloud-based computing, and the Internet of Things (IoT) (Steinwandter et al., 2019). ML is a subfield of AI in computer science where results from data sets of past actions, decisions, or observations are used for designing algorithm-based training in a statistical and logical approach to improve predictions or future decision-making (Mohri et al., 2018). ML can use training data sets from each unit operation to construct a binary classifier to efficiently predict whether the final product will be within or out of a specified quality threshold (Mowbray et al., 2021). This way, an informed decision can be made to save time and resources by not proceeding with downstream processing. ML-based automation can quickly find the causes of process deviations, select the appropriate analytical methods, forecast system behavior, and be capable of reasoning and decision-making, dealing with uncertainties and imprecise information (Patel and Shah, 2022). ML-based modeling has applications in soft sensors, chemometrics models, selecting mode of operation, multivariate data analysis, multivariate statistical process control, estimation of process state, dynamic simulations, and predictive control. A range of ML algorithm-based models (Mowbray et al., 2021, Rathore et al., 2022a, Rathore et al., 2022b) and a few hybrid models have been utilized and integrated into bioprocess systems for assessing complex relations of CPPs and CQAs (Bayrak et al., 2018, Krippl et al., 2020, Narayanan et al., 2020).

However, there is still a lack of information on data accessibility and management, selection of modeling techniques, detection methods, prediction capabilities, and construction of hybrid models. Despite the growth in this domain, due to large time scale and parameter variations, lack of cyber integrated (i.e., IoT, cloud computing) systems, auto-calibrated on-site sensors, and complex biological environment, complete automatization for real-time monitoring and comprehensive process control at large scale is still a bottleneck in the bioprocess industry. Hence, it is essential to know the state of technology, harnessable potential, and its limitations to fill the gaps precluding its current application in the bioprocessing industry. We summarize and provide an elaborated perspective on the current status, its limitations, and future stance of existing ML-based solutions for bioprocess systems- biofuel production, biopharmaceutical production, and biological wastewater treatment.

----------------

Machine learning-model design
-----------------------------

At the end of the 21st century, there have been many advancements in developing computer-aided system designs, architectures, computer vision, and signal processing. ML is considered a field of study that allows computers to learn, self-teach, analyze data, and estimate after initially being programmed without needing explicit programming at every stage. ML has established significant applications in the bioprocess industry, with its influence demonstrating domain understanding and innovation,

Machine learning algorithms
---------------------------

In the present scenario, the application of ML in the bioprocess industry addresses the fast-paced and domain-specific approaches followed by different modeling techniques. Many recent modeling approaches applied to biopharmaceutical, biofuel processes, and soft sensors-integrated control were used for the real-time estimation of CPPs/CQAs. These are based on real-time estimations and server-designed online parameter estimations. Moreover, multivariate data analysis – does the end-process

Application of ML in the bioprocess industry
--------------------------------------------

ML algorithms have been used with increasing regularity to develop a deeper understanding of bioprocesses. Constrictive investigation in this area needs nexus between biochemical engineering and computer science. This section discusses the recent progress and encountered challenges in applying ML to improve bioprocesses (biofuel production, pharmaceuticals, and fermentation process) and the various modeling tools.

Research needs and future perspectives
--------------------------------------

Though much has been investigated and applied for implementing ML in the bioprocessing industry, it is still considered in the early development and use stage. A lot depends on proper storage and data management for the successful application of ML in the enterprise continuum. Furthermore, the points mentioned below are directed toward the research needs and the demands of implementing ML in the bioprocess industry:
*   1.Due to the lesser-known availability of authentic on-site datasets, feedback 

Conclusions
-----------

This review addresses the commonly used ML algorithms for data-driven modeling to assist in process control for the bioproduction industry and wastewater treatment. It is evident from the extensive study that a lot depends on data management, interrogation, and exploitation for converting basic research and industrial raw data into interpretable shared knowledge databases. IoT, and cloud computing, incorporating multi-parameter sensors and hybrid ML algorithms, will enable real-time monitoring

*   ### [Response surface methodology for process optimization in livestock wastewater treatment: A review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024063576)

2024, Heliyon  Show abstract With increasing demand for meat and dairy products, the volume of wastewater generated from the livestock industry has become a significant environmental concern. The treatment of livestock wastewater (LWW) is a challenging process that involves removing nutrients, organic matter, pathogens, and other pollutants from livestock manure and urine. In response to this challenge, researchers have developed and investigated different biological, physical, and chemical treatment technologies that perform better upon optimization. Optimization of LWW handling processes can help improve the efficacy and sustainability of treatment systems as well as minimize environmental impacts and associated costs. Response surface methodology (RSM) as an optimization approach can effectively optimize operational parameters that affect process performance. This review article summarizes the main steps of RSM, recent applications of RSM in LWW treatment, highlights the advantages and limitations of this technique, and provides recommendations for future research and practice, including its cost-effectiveness, accuracy, and ability to improve treatment efficiency.    
*   ### [A review on machine learning approaches for microalgae cultivation systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0010482524003329)

2024, Computers in Biology and Medicine Citation Excerpt : 
By adjusting these parameters in real time, the ML algorithm can better adapt to changing conditions and provide predictive insights for future actions. This dynamic approach enables more accurate modeling of complex processes and facilitates proactive decision-making as demonstrated by [98]. As mentioned earlier, DL algorithms necessitate extensive datasets to capture the complex relationship between features and responses effectively.   Show abstract Microalgae plays a crucial role in biomass production within aquatic environments and are increasingly recognized for their potential in generating biofuels, biomaterials, bioactive compounds, and bio-based chemicals. This growing significance is driven by the need to address imminent global challenges such as food and fuel shortages. Enhancing the value chain of bio-based products necessitates the implementation of an advanced screening and monitoring system. This system is crucial for tailoring and optimizing the cultivation conditions, ensuring the lucrative and efficient production of the final desired product. This, in turn, underscores the necessity for robust predictive models to accurately emulate algae growth in different conditions during the initial cultivation phase and simulate their subsequent processing in the downstream stage. In pursuit of these objectives, diverse mechanistic and machine learning-based methods have been independently employed to model and optimize microalgae processes. This review article thoroughly examines the techniques delineated in the literature for modeling, predicting, and monitoring microalgal biomass across various applications such as bioenergy, pharmaceuticals, and the food industry. While highlighting the merits and limitations of each method, we delve into the realm of newly emerging hybrid approaches and conduct an exhaustive survey of this evolving methodology. The challenges currently impeding the practical implementation of hybrid techniques are explored, and drawing inspiration from successful applications in other machine-learning-assisted fields, we review various plausible solutions to overcome these obstacles.    
*   ### [Machine learning-based characterization of hydrochar from biomass: Implications for sustainable energy and material production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0016236123010803)

2023, Fuel  Show abstract Hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) is a process that converts biomass into versatile hydrochar without the need for prior drying. The physicochemical properties of hydrochar are influenced by biomass properties and processing parameters, making it challenging to optimize for specific applications through trial-and-error experiments. To save time and money, machine learning can be used to develop a model that characterizes hydrochar produced from different biomass sources under varying reaction processing parameters. Thus, this study aims to develop an inclusive model to characterize hydrochar using a database covering a range of biomass types and reaction processing parameters. The quality and quantity of hydrochar are predicted using two models (decision tree regression and support vector regression). The decision tree regression model outperforms the support vector regression model in terms of forecast accuracy (R 2>0.88, RMSE<6.848, and MAE<4.718). Using an evolutionary algorithm, optimum inputs are identified based on cost functions provided by the selected model to optimize hydrochar for energy production, soil amendment, and pollutant adsorption, resulting in hydrochar yields of 84.31%, 84.91%, and 80.40%, respectively. The feature importance analysis reveals that biomass ash/carbon content and operating temperature are the primary factors affecting hydrochar production in the HTC process.

# Review on machine learning-based bioprocess optimization, monitoring, and control systems

### Affiliations

# Review on machine learning-based bioprocess optimization, monitoring, and control systems

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

Machine Learning is quickly becoming an impending game changer for transforming big data thrust from the bioprocessing industry into actionable output. However, the complex data set from bioprocess, lagging cyber-integrated sensor system, and issues with storage scalability limit machine learning real-time application. Hence, it is imperative to know the state of technology to address prevailing issues. This review first gives an insight into the basic understanding of the machine learning domain and discusses its complexities for more comprehensive applications. Followed by an outline of how relevant machine learning models are for statistical and logical analysis of the enormous datasets generated to control bioprocess operations. Then this review critically discusses the current knowledge, its limitations, and future aspects in different subfields of the bioprocessing industry. Further, this review discusses the prospects of adopting a hybrid method to dovetail different modeling strategies, cyber-networking, and integrated sensors to develop new digital biotechnologies.

**Keywords:**
Biofuels; Biological water treatment; Biopharmaceuticals; Machine learning; Modeling.

# Harnessing Machine Learning for Advanced Bioprocess Development: From Data-Driven Optimization to Real-Time Monitoring

Modern bioprocess development, driven by advanced analytical techniques, digitalization, and automation, generates extensive experimental data valuable for process optimization—ML methods to analyze these large datasets, enabling efficient exploration of design spaces in bioprocessing. Specifically, ML techniques have been applied in strain engineering, bioprocess optimization, scale-up, and real-time monitoring and control. Conventional sensors in chemical and bioprocessing measure basic variables like pressure, temperature, and pH. However, measuring the concentration of other chemical species typically requires slower, invasive at-line or off-line methods. By leveraging the interaction of monochromatic light with molecules, Raman spectroscopy allows for real-time sensing and differentiation of chemical species through their unique spectral profiles.

Applying ML and DL methods to process Raman spectral data holds great potential for enhancing the prediction accuracy and robustness of analyte concentrations in complex mixtures. Preprocessing Raman spectra and employing advanced regression models have outperformed traditional methods, particularly in managing high-dimensional data with overlapping spectral contributions. Challenges such as the curse of dimensionality and limited training data are addressed through methods like synthetic data augmentation and feature importance analysis. Additionally, integrating predictions from multiple models and using low-dimensional representations through techniques like Variational Autoencoders can further improve the robustness and accuracy of regression models. This approach, tested across diverse datasets and target variables, demonstrates significant advancements in the monitoring and controlling bioprocesses.

**Application of Machine Learning in Bioprocess Development:**

ML has profoundly impacted bioprocess development, particularly in strain selection and engineering stages. ML leverages large, complex datasets to optimize biocatalyst design and metabolic pathway predictions, enhancing productivity and efficiency. Ensemble learning and neural networks integrate genomic data with bioprocess parameters, enabling predictive modeling and strain improvement. Challenges include extrapolation limitations and the need for diverse datasets for non-model organisms. ML tools such as the Automated Recommendation Tool for Synthetic Biology aid in iterative design cycles, advancing synthetic biology applications. Overall, ML offers versatile tools crucial for accelerating bioprocess development and innovation.

**Bioprocess Optimization Using Machine Learning:**

ML is pivotal in optimizing bioprocesses, focusing on enhancing titers, rates, and yields (TRY) through precise control of physicochemical parameters. ML techniques like support vector machine (SVM) regression and Gaussian process (GP) regression predict optimal conditions for enzymatic activities and media composition. Applications span from optimizing fermentation parameters for various products to predicting light distribution in algae cultivation. ML models, including [artificial neural networks](https://www.marktechpost.com/2024/12/10/understanding-the-artificial-neural-networks-anns/) (ANNs), are employed for complex data analysis from microscopy images, aiding in microfluidic-based high-throughput bioprocess development. Challenges include scaling ML models from lab to industrial production and addressing variability and complexity inherent on larger scales.

**ML in Process Analytical Technology (PAT) for Bioprocess Monitoring and Control:**

In bioprocess development for commercial production, Process Analytical Technology (PAT) ensures compliance with regulatory standards like those set by the FDA and EMA. ML techniques are pivotal in PAT for monitoring critical process parameters (CPPs) and maintaining biopharmaceutical products’ critical quality attributes (CQAs). Using ML models such as ANNs and support vector machines (SVMs), soft sensors enable real-time prediction of process variables where direct measurement is challenging. These models, integrated into digital twins, facilitate predictive process behavior analysis and optimization. Challenges include data transferability and adaptation to new plant conditions, driving research towards enhanced transfer learning techniques in bioprocessing applications.

![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAAAAAP///yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7)
![](https://lh7-us.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXe57XLO11Y40rQM8oBszfmyauvp_GNTRApyJY8SHTe-VZ2RxdpkOYklHu3PlLCBz3sKf_tggPDKZKrjcO00_s0TfaEJkvutaQbX8tC1yGvX8_akqFaBJ2A8cIebZJ81mWAgPx-4xlhflz6Gx9vHrgyPfXw?key=SmVMO9dCz-w4CACSi77r2A)

**Enhancing Raman Spectroscopy in Bioprocessing through Machine Learning:**

Traditional online sensors are limited to basic variables like pressure, temperature, and pH in bioprocessing and chemical processing while measuring other chemical species often requires slower, invasive methods. Raman spectroscopy offers real-time sensing capabilities using monochromatic light to distinguish molecules based on their unique spectral profiles. ML and DL methods enhance Raman spectroscopy by modeling relationships between spectral profiles and analyte concentrations. Techniques include preprocessing of spectra, feature selection, and augmentation of training data to improve prediction accuracy and robustness for monitoring multiple variables crucial in bioprocess control. Successful applications include predicting concentrations of biomolecules like glucose, lactate, and product titers in real time.

**Conclusion:**

ML is increasingly integral in bioprocess development, evolving from individual tools to comprehensive frameworks covering entire process pipelines. Embracing open-source methodologies and databases is crucial for rapid advancement, fostering collaboration and data accessibility. ML facilitates the exploration of vast unanalyzed datasets, promising new strategies in bioprocess development. Transfer learning and ensemble methods address challenges like overfitting, underfitting, and data scarcity. As ML methods like deep learning and reinforcement learning continue to advance with computational capabilities, they offer transformative potential for optimizing bioprocesses and shaping a data-driven future in biotechnology.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Machine learning analyzes extensive bioprocess data to uncover complex patterns and optimize variables for enhanced productivity, significantly accelerating development cycles in biotechnology.
ML techniques streamline strain selection by predicting optimal genetic modifications and metabolic pathways, enhancing biocatalyst efficiency for various bioproducts in real-world applications.
Machine learning is applied in bioprocess monitoring, control, and optimization, facilitating real-time adjustments in parameters to ensure product quality and process efficiency.
